activity_metadata.objective: Students will determine Malala Yousafzai's point of view and purpose in "I Am Malala" and analyze how she conveys them through descriptive details and word choice.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the Your Turn Activity section of the Author's Purpose and Point of View skill lesson for "I Am Malala: The Girl Who Stood Up for Education and Was Shot by the Taliban." It offers a scaffolded sequence that guides students through analyzing how an author conveys purpose and point of view through descriptive details, word choice, and contrasts.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity supports the end-of-unit Extended Writing Project, where students write a literary analysis. By practicing how to identify and explain how authors convey point of view through specific textual details and word choices, students develop the analytical skills they will need to write about how authors develop character qualities in their literary analysis essays.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This activity connects to the unit's essential question "Which qualities of character matter most?" by having students analyze how Malala reveals her character through her point of view. Her appreciation for cultural freedom, her dreams of professional opportunities, and her complex feelings about both cities reveal qualities like hope, resilience, and determination—helping students understand how authors show character through perspective.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Excerpt from Chapter 13: The Diary of Gul Makai
Paragraphs 14-15

(14) Adam took us to Islamabad. It was the first time I had ever visited. Islamabad was a beautiful place with nice white bungalows and broad roads, though it has none of the natural beauty of Swat. We saw the Red Mosque where the siege had taken place, the buildings of the Parliament House and the Presidency, where Zardari now lived. General Musharraf was in exile in London.

(15) We went to shops where I bought school books and Adam bought me DVDs of American TV programs like Ugly Betty, which was about a girl with big braces and a big heart. I loved it and dreamed of one day going to New York and working on a magazine like her. We visited the Lok Virsa museum, and it was a joy to celebrate our national heritage once again. Our own museum in Swat had closed. On the steps outside an old man was selling popcorn. He was a Pashtun like us, and when my father asked if he was from Islamabad he replied, “Do you think Islamabad can ever belong to us Pashtuns?” He said he came from Mohmand, one of the tribal areas, but had to flee because of a military operation. I saw tears in my parents’ eyes.
exit_ticket: What is the author's purpose in telling readers about Islamabad in paragraph 14?
Which of the following best describes the author's point of view in paragraph 15?
